questions.question: What is declaration merging in TypeScript?

choices.a: Combining multiple .ts files into one during compilation
choices.b: TypeScript's ability to combine multiple declarations of the same name into a single definition
choices.d: A compiler optimization for reducing bundle size

questions.question: What is module augmentation and how do you use it?

choices.a: Adding new exports to an existing npm package by editing its source
choices.b: Using `declare module 'module-name'` inside a regular `.ts` file to add types to an existing module's exports
choices.c: Overwriting a module's existing types with your own
choices.d: Module augmentation is only possible for modules you own

questions.question: What is the difference between `import type` and `import` in TypeScript?

choices.a: `import type` is slower because TypeScript makes an extra check
choices.b: `import type` imports only type information — it is completely erased at compile time and never generates import statements in the JS output
choices.c: They are identical — TypeScript auto-detects whether a value import is type-only
choices.d: `import type` requires the `isolatedModules` flag to work

questions.explanation:
`import type` was introduced in TypeScript 3.8. It guarantees that the import is ERASED at compile time:

```typescript
import type { User } from './types'; // type-only import — no JS output
import { loadUser } from './api';    // regular import — becomes JS require/import
```

Why this matters:
• `isolatedModules` mode (used by Babel, esbuild, SWC) cannot decide whether an import is type-only without TypeScript. `import type` makes it explicit.
• Prevents circular dependency issues caused by type-only import chains
• Makes dependencies clearer — if you remove all type usage of an import, `import type` can flag it

In TS 4.5+, you can also do: `import { type User, loadUser } from './module'` for inline type imports.

questions.question: What are ambient declarations (`.d.ts` files)?

choices.a: TypeScript files that run at compile time only, similar to macros
choices.b: Declaration files that describe the shape of existing JavaScript — no implementation, only type information used by TypeScript
choices.c: Files that augment the global scope
choices.d: Compiled output files generated by TypeScript

questions.question: What is the `paths` option in tsconfig.json used for?

choices.a: It sets the output directory for compiled files
choices.b: It defines module path aliases, mapping import paths to file system locations
choices.c: It configures which files are included in compilation
choices.d: It sets the Node.js `NODE_PATH` for module resolution

questions.question: What is the `isolatedModules` TypeScript option and why do tools like Babel/esbuild require it?

choices.a: It compiles each file separately without full type checking, catching patterns that require full program knowledge (like re-exporting types)
choices.b: It enables tree-shaking in the TypeScript compiler
choices.c: It prevents importing from other files in the project
choices.d: It is only relevant for libraries published to npm

questions.explanation:
`isolatedModules: true` makes TypeScript warn about patterns that are unsafe when files are transpiled IN ISOLATION (without full program knowledge):

The problem: tools like Babel, SWC, and esbuild transpile each file INDEPENDENTLY — they don't know if an import is a value or a type. This breaks:

```typescript
// PROBLEMATIC with isolatedModules:
export { User } from './types';  // Is User a type or a value? Babel can't tell!

// SAFE alternatives:
export type { User } from './types';  // explicit type re-export
```

With `isolatedModules: true`, TypeScript:
• Requires `const enum` to be replaced by regular `enum` or inline values
• Requires type-only exports to use `export type`
• Requires type-only imports to use `import type` or inline `type` keyword

Nx projects using esbuild or Babel should always have `isolatedModules: true`.
